morphology.summary: A Latinate adjective whose present form is not safely decomposed as English sine plus wax.

etymology.summary: The word comes through Middle French from Latin sincerus; the popular wax story is treated as folk etymology rather than origin.

semantic_drift.summary: The meaning remains centered on genuineness while also supporting moral, emotional, and communicative readings.

developments.description: Material or general genuineness supports inward sincerity.
A memorable folk explanation competes with the attested lineage.

present_usage.summary: Modern sincere usually means genuine, earnest, or truly meant.

senses.definition: Genuine in feeling, expression, or intention.

examples.text: Her apology sounded sincere because it named the harm clearly.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

senses.definition: Done earnestly or with real intention, even if imperfect.

examples.text: It was a sincere attempt, though the solution failed.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

cultural_frame.summary: The word belongs to trust, apology, authenticity, and folk-etymology correction frames.

frames.label: authentic intention
frames.description: Frames speech or action as matching inner commitment.

frames.label: folk etymology warning
frames.description: Frames the word as a caution against appealing to attractive false origins.

pragmatics.summary: Sincere can reassure, persuade, evaluate trust, or challenge fakery.

stances.label: trust-building
stances.description: Presents a speaker or action as authentic.

stances.label: skepticism
stances.description: Questions whether a display is merely performed.

expansion.paragraph: Sincere is useful for EI because it shows why etymology must be handled carefully. The attested path runs through Middle French and Latin forms associated with genuineness or purity, while the popular without-wax explanation is a folk etymology. Modern use concerns authentic feeling, intention, or effort, not wax.

trace.contribution: The word comes through Middle French from Latin sincerus; the popular wax story is treated as folk etymology rather than origin.
The meaning remains centered on genuineness while also supporting moral, emotional, and communicative readings.
Sincere can reassure, persuade, evaluate trust, or challenge fakery.

capsule_summary: A genuineness word that doubles as a cautionary example against attractive but false folk etymology.
